accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Start by deciding whether this is emergency, cancer-risk, or likely low-risk benign disease.

  • Take symptom history (pain pattern, bloating/fullness, appetite and urinary symptoms).
  • Examine abdomen for mass, tenderness, guarding/peritonism, and distension; perform pelvic exam where appropriate.
  • Request transvaginal ultrasound first line (or transabdominal if TVUS not possible) and classify morphology/size.
  • Postmenopausal: check CA125 for any ovarian mass and calculate RMI.
  • Premenopausal: check CA125 for complex/suspicious cysts (not routine for clearly simple cysts).

RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index, postmenopausal): RMI = U x M x CA125.

U (ultrasound score) is based on these features: multilocular cyst, solid areas, metastases, ascites, bilateral lesions. U = 0 (no features), U = 1 (one feature), U = 3 (two or more features).

M (menopausal score): 1 premenopausal, 3 postmenopausal. RMI >200 indicates higher malignancy risk.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
Premenopausal women

  • Simple cyst <5 cm: no routine follow-up.
  • Simple cyst 5-7 cm: repeat ultrasound at 6 months.
  • Simple cyst >7 cm, or persistent/enlarging cyst: refer.
  • Complex cyst: refer (urgency based on symptoms and malignant features).

Postmenopausal women

  • Simple unilateral unilocular cyst ≤3 cm: no routine follow-up.
  • Simple cyst >3 to <5 cm with normal CA125 and no suspicious features: repeat ultrasound at 6 months.
  • If unchanged or smaller at 12 months and no new concerning features: discharge.
  • Cyst >5 cm, persistent cyst, symptoms, raised CA125, or suspicious morphology: refer.
  • Any complex cyst: refer (urgent if malignant features/high RMI).

Give clear safety-net advice for worsening pain, vomiting, fever, increasing distension, or collapse.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Hospital admission (same day)

  • Suspected torsion.
  • Suspected ruptured/haemorrhagic cyst with acute abdomen.
  • Haemodynamic instability or uncontrolled acute pain.

Urgent suspicion of cancer (USOC)

  • Postmenopausal RMI >200.
  • Suspicious malignant ultrasound features (solid areas/papillary projections, ascites, bilateral complex mass, metastatic features).
  • Strong overall clinical suspicion of ovarian malignancy.

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • Symptomatic complex cyst not meeting USOC threshold but needing rapid specialist review.
  • Persistent concerning symptoms with indeterminate imaging.

Routine (outpatient)

  • Premenopausal simple cyst >7 cm.
  • Persistent/enlarging cyst on interval scans.
  • Postmenopausal cyst >5 cm or persistent cyst needing specialist review.

Include in referral

  • Menopausal status, symptom timeline, examination findings.
  • Ultrasound morphology, size, and laterality.
  • CA125 and RMI (if postmenopausal).
  • Previous imaging and progression.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html:
• Premenopausal simple cysts <5 cm in otherwise well patients.
  • Postmenopausal simple unilateral unilocular cysts ≤3 cm with no concerning features.
  • Selected postmenopausal simple cysts >3 to <5 cm with normal CA125 and stable interval imaging.
